summary: Joel Spolsky briefly explains why Fog Creek Software chose to reimplement SQL Server Mirroring rather than use Microsoft's built-in solution. He links to their systems administrator's detailed technical explanation of why the off-the-shelf technology didn't fit their needs. Joel reinforces this with his long-held philosophy that great teams often need to build their own solutions rather than relying on third-party tools. The post is essentially a short endorsement of the 'Not Invented Here' syndrome when practiced by strong engineering teams.
key_points: SQL Server Mirroring sounded ideal on paper but failed to meet Fog Creek's actual operational needs
Great engineering teams sometimes need to build custom solutions rather than adopt existing tools
Joel connects this specific technical decision back to his broader 2001 philosophy about self-reliance in software development
money_quotes: When you're working on a really, really good team with great programmers, everybody else's code, frankly, is bug-infested garbage, and nobody else knows how to ship on time.

When you're a cordon bleu chef and you need fresh lavender, you grow it yourself instead of buying it in the farmers' market, because sometimes they don't have fresh lavender or they have old lavender which they pass off as fresh.
Even though the SQL Server Mirroring technology sounds like an ideal fit at first, it is easy to see how it doesn't really suit our needs.
key_insight: Strong engineering teams are justified in rebuilding existing solutions when off-the-shelf tools don't truly fit their needs, even if it looks like reinventing the wheel from the outside.
